---
title: "Synthetic Control Teaching Data"
---

# Scope

This note lists datasets that are especially useful for teaching synthetic control methods, based on package documentation and online materials, with particular emphasis on `tidysynth` and related R packages [@tidysynth-package; @synth-package; @augsynth-package; @synthdid-package; @scpi-package; @sctools-package].

# Short Answer

If you want one dataset for a first synthetic control lesson, use `tidysynth::smoking`. It is bundled, well documented, tied to the canonical California Proposition 99 application, and easy to teach in a tidy workflow [@tidysynth-smoking-doc; @abadie2010synthetic].

If you want a compact teaching sequence, use:

1. `Synth::synth.data` for the mechanics.
2. `tidysynth::smoking` for the first real application.
3. `Synth::basque` for the original comparative case-study logic.
4. `augsynth::kansas` or `synthdid::california_prop99` for modern extensions.
5. `scpi::scpi_germany` or `microsynth::seattledmi` for advanced sessions on inference or nonstandard data structures.

# Best Beginner Datasets

## `tidysynth::smoking`

This is the cleanest beginner dataset I found for teaching vanilla synthetic control in R [@tidysynth-smoking-doc].

- Substantive case: California's Proposition 99 tobacco-control program.
- Data shape: 1,209 rows, 39 states, 1970 to 2000.
- Outcome: cigarette sales per capita, with a small set of intuitive predictors.
- Why it teaches well: one treated unit, one clear intervention date, familiar policy story, and easy placebo exercises.
- Why it is especially useful: students can stay inside a tidy pipeline and still learn the core SCM logic [@tidysynth-package].

This is also the same canonical application used in the original JASA paper, which makes it useful for linking software, plots, and published results [@abadie2010synthetic].

## `Synth::synth.data`

This is an artificial toy dataset bundled with `Synth` specifically to demonstrate the method [@synth-synthdata-doc].

- Substantive case: none; it is synthetic by design.
- Data shape: 8 units over 21 time periods with 3 predictors and 1 treated unit.
- Why it teaches well: it strips away real-world narrative complexity and lets you focus on `dataprep`, predictor balance, weights, and gap plots.
- Main limitation: it is too artificial for teaching substantive interpretation or placebo logic.

Use this when the goal is to explain the optimizer and data-prep structure before moving to a real application.

## `Synth::basque`

This is the classic Basque Country comparative case study bundled in `Synth` [@synth-basque-doc; @abadie2003economic].

- Substantive case: the economic effects of political conflict in the Basque Country.
- Data shape: 17 Spanish regions from 1955 to 1997.
- Outcome: per-capita GDP, plus sectoral and demographic predictors.
- Why it teaches well: long pre-treatment history, canonical SCM story, and clear comparison between treated region and weighted donor regions.
- Main limitation: the economic and historical context is richer, but less immediately intuitive than the California smoking example.

This is the best second dataset after `smoking` if you want students to see the original comparative case-study use case.

# Good Datasets For Teaching Extensions

## `synthdid::california_prop99`

The `synthdid` package includes a simplified Proposition 99 dataset designed for synthetic difference-in-differences workflows [@synthdid-california-doc; @arkhangelsky2021synthetic].

- Substantive case: same California tobacco-control intervention.
- Why it teaches well: lets you reuse a familiar case while changing the estimator.
- Data shape: minimal columns and a straightforward path into matrix form.
- Best use: showing how SCM-style weighting and DiD-style differencing relate.

This is particularly useful if you want to teach method comparison without changing the substantive example.

## `augsynth::kansas`

The `augsynth` package bundles a Kansas state tax-cut panel from 1990 to 2016 [@augsynth-kansas-doc; @benmichael2021augmented].

- Substantive case: state-level economic effects of Kansas tax cuts.
- Data shape: quarterly state panel with many economic indicators.
- Why it teaches well: useful for showing why exact pre-treatment fit is often hard and why augmentation or bias correction becomes attractive.
- Best use: teaching the transition from original SCM to augmented SCM.

This is a better extension dataset than a beginner dataset because the panel and predictor structure are richer and less transparent at first pass.

## `scpi::scpi_germany`

The `scpi` package includes a replication dataset for German reunification [@scpi-germany-doc; @scpi-package].

- Substantive case: the economic impact of German reunification.
- Data shape: 17 OECD countries from 1960 to 2003.
- Why it teaches well: very strong for inference discussions because `scpi` is built around prediction intervals and uncertainty quantification.
- Best use: advanced modules on inference, interval estimation, and sensitivity to weight constraints.

This is a strong choice when the lesson is not just point estimation, but how uncertainty should be communicated in SCM work.

## `SCtools::alcohol`

`SCtools` bundles a world alcohol-consumption panel used as an SCM case study around Russian policy changes after 2003 [@sctools-alcohol-doc; @sctools-package].

- Substantive case: alcohol policy and consumption in the Russian Federation.
- Why it teaches well: `SCtools` is useful for placebo generation, MSPE-ratio checks, and other post-estimation diagnostics layered on top of `Synth`.
- Best use: teaching inference graphics and placebo workflows after students already know the core estimator.

This is a good teaching dataset if the real objective is diagnostics rather than first exposure to SCM itself.

## `microsynth::seattledmi`

The `microsynth` package includes block-level Seattle crime data for a Drug Market Intervention in 2013 [@microsynth-seattledmi-doc; @robbins2017microsynth].

- Substantive case: a neighborhood crime intervention in Seattle.
- Data shape: 9,642 blocks with quarterly observations and multiple crime outcomes.
- Why it teaches well: shows how the synthetic-control idea extends beyond country or state panels into micro- or meso-level intervention settings.
- Main limitation: too large and complex for a first lesson.

This works best in an advanced session that asks when standard aggregate-unit SCM stops being the right mental model.

# What To Use For Different Teaching Goals

## If The Goal Is To Teach The Original Algorithm

Use:

- `Synth::synth.data`
- `tidysynth::smoking`
- `Synth::basque`

That sequence moves from mechanics to a simple real case to the canonical comparative case-study application.

## If The Goal Is To Teach Modern SCM Variants

Use:

- `tidysynth::smoking` for baseline SCM
- `augsynth::kansas` for augmented SCM
- `synthdid::california_prop99` for synthetic DiD
- `scpi::scpi_germany` for uncertainty quantification

That keeps the lesson close to package workflows students can actually run.

## If The Goal Is To Teach Diagnostics

Use:

- `tidysynth::smoking` for placebo and trend plots
- `SCtools::alcohol` for placebo and MSPE-ratio workflows
- `Synth::basque` if you want the original case-study framing

# Recommended Teaching Sequence

For a short workshop or note series, I would use the following order:

1. `Synth::synth.data` to explain the setup, donor pool, predictors, and weights.
2. `tidysynth::smoking` to teach a complete end-to-end SCM workflow.
3. `Synth::basque` to connect the method to the classic comparative case-study literature.
4. `augsynth::kansas` to show why imperfect fit pushes analysts toward augmentation.
5. `synthdid::california_prop99` to compare SCM and synthetic DiD on a familiar case.
6. `scpi::scpi_germany` or `microsynth::seattledmi` for advanced topics.

# Minimal R Pointers

```r
library(tidysynth)
data(smoking)

library(Synth)
data(synth.data)
data(basque)

library(augsynth)
data(kansas)

library(synthdid)
data(california_prop99)

library(scpi)
data(scpi_germany)

library(microsynth)
data(seattledmi)

library(SCtools)
data(alcohol)
```

# Bottom Line

After searching online package documentation, `tidysynth::smoking` stands out as the best single teaching dataset for synthetic control in R because it is canonical, bundled, tidy, and easy to explain. `Synth::basque` is the best follow-on dataset for teaching the original comparative case-study tradition, while `augsynth::kansas`, `synthdid::california_prop99`, `scpi::scpi_germany`, `SCtools::alcohol`, and `microsynth::seattledmi` are the best next step once the lesson shifts from vanilla SCM to diagnostics, augmentation, inference, or nonstandard data structures [@tidysynth-smoking-doc; @synth-basque-doc; @augsynth-kansas-doc; @synthdid-california-doc; @scpi-germany-doc; @sctools-alcohol-doc; @microsynth-seattledmi-doc].
